audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a phone conversation between Alex, an employee at a company called JPNW, and Martha, who wants to work as a trainee at the company.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Alex.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: It's Martha Clines here.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: James White gave me your number.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I hope you don't mind me calling you.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Of course not.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: How are you, Martha?
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Good, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I'm ringing because I need a bit of advice.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: What about?
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: The training you did at JPNW a few years ago.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I'm applying for the same thing.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Yes, I did mine in 2014. Best thing I ever did, I'm still working there.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Alex did his training in 2014. So 2014 has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Alex.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: It's Martha Clines here.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: James White gave me your number.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I hope you don't mind me calling you.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Of course not.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: How are you, Martha?
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Good, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I'm ringing because I need a bit of advice.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: What about?
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: The training you did at JPNW a few years ago.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I'm applying for the same thing.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Yes, I did mine in 2014. Best thing I ever did.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I'm still working there.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: What are you doing?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Well, now I work in the customer services department.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: But I did my initial training in Finance.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I stayed there for the first two years, and then moved to where I am now.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: That's the same department I'm applying for.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Did you enjoy it?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Uh. I was pretty nervous to begin with.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I didn't do well in my exams at school, and I was really worried because I failed maths.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: But it didn't actually matter, because I did lots of courses on the job.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Did you get a diploma at the end of your trainee period?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I'm hoping to do the one in business skills.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Yes, that sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I took the one on IT skills, but I wish I'd done that one instead.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: OK, that's good to know.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Um. What about the other trainees?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: How did you get on with them?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: There were about 20 of us who started at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: And we were all around the same age.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I was 18, and there was only one person younger than me, who was 17.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: The rest were between 18 and 20.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I made some good friends.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I've heard lots of good things about the training at JPNW.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: It seems like there are a lot of opportunities there.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Yeah, definitely.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Because of its size, you can work in loads of different areas within the organization.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: What about pay?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: I know you get a lower minimum wage than regular employees.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: That's right, which isn't great.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: But you get the same number of days' holiday as everyone else, and the pay goes up massively if they offer you a job at the end of the training period.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Yeah, but I'm not doing it for the money.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: It's the experience I think will be really useful.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Everyone says by the end of the year you gain so much confidence.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: You're right.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: That's the most useful part about it.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: There's a lot of variety too.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: You're given lots of different things to do.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I enjoyed it all.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I didn't even mind the studying.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Do you have to spend any time in college?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Yes, 1 day each month.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: So you get lots of support from both your tutor and your manager.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's good.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: And the company is easy to get to, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Yes, it's very close to the train station, so the location's a real advantage.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the phone conversation, you have some time to look at questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Have you got a date for your interview yet?
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Yes, it's on the 23rd of this month.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: So long as you're well prepared, there's nothing to worry about.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Everyone's very friendly.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: I am not sure what I should wear.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: What do you think?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Nothing too casual, like jeans for example.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: If you've got a nice jacket, wear that with a skirt or trousers.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Any other tips?
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Um. Well, I know it's really obvious, but arrive in plenty of time.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: They hate people who are late.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: So make sure you know exactly where you have to get to, and one other useful piece of advice my manager told me before I had the interview for this job is to smile.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Even if you feel terrified, it makes people respond better to you.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Ha, I'll have to practice doing that in the mirror.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: Yeah, well, good luck.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Let me know if you need any more information.
audioscript.speaker: ALEX

audioscript.text: Thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: MARTHA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Advice for interview:

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman called Annie, who works at the Snow Center in New Zealand, welcoming a group of visitors to the center.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Welcome to the snow center.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: My name's Annie.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I hope you enjoyed the bus trip from the airport.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: We've certainly got plenty of snow today.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Well, you've come to New Zealand's premier snow and ski center, and we've a whole load of activities for you during your week here.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Most visitors come here for the cross country skiing.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Where you're on fairly flat ground for most of the time, rather than going down steep mountainsides.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: There are marked trails, but you can also leave these and go off on your own, and that's an experience not to be missed.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: You can go at your own speed.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: It's great aerobic exercise if you really push yourself, or if you prefer, you can just glide gently along and enjoy the beautiful scenery.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: This afternoon you'll be going on a dog sled trip.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: You may have seen our dogs on TV recently, racing in the Winter Sled festival.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: If you want, you can have your own team for the afternoon, and learn how to drive them.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Following behind our leader on the trail, or if you prefer, you can just sit back in the sled and enjoy the ride as a passenger.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: At the weekend, we have the team relay event, and you're all welcome to join in.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: We have a local school coming along, and a lot of the teachers are taking part too.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Participation rather than winning is the main focus, and there's a medal for everyone who takes part.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Participants are in teams of two to four, and each team must complete four laps of the course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: For your final expedition, you'll head off to Mount Frenner, wearing a pair of special snowshoes which allow you to walk on top of the snow.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: This is an area where miners once searched for gold.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Though there are very few traces of their work left now.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: When the snow melts in summer, the mountain slopes are carpeted in flowers and plants.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: It's a long ascent, though not too steep.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: And walkers generally take a couple of days to get to the summit and return.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: You'll spend the night in our hut halfway up the mountain.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: That's included in your package for the stay.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: It's got cooking facilities, firewood and water for drinking.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: For washing, we recommend you use melted snow though to conserve supplies.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: We can take your luggage up on our snowmobile for you for just $10 a person.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: The hut has cooking facilities, so you can make a hot meal in the evening and morning, but you need to take your own food.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: The weather on Mount Frenner can be very stormy.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: In that case, stay in the hut.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Generally the storms don't last long.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Don't stress about getting back here to the center in time to catch the airport bus.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: They'll probably not be running anyway.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: We do have an emergency locator beacon in the hut, but only use that if it's a real emergency, like if someone's ill or injured.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, let me tell you something about the different ski trails you can follow during your stay here.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Highland Trail's directly accessible from where we are now.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: This trail's been designed to give first-timers an experience they'll enjoy regardless of their age or skill.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: But it's also ideal for experts to practice their technique.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Then there's Pine Trail.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: If you're nervous about skiing, leave this one to the experts.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: You follow a steep valley looking right down on the river below.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Scary.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: But if you fully master the techniques needed for hills, it's great fun.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Stony Trail's a good choice once you've got a general idea of the basics.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: There are one or two tricky sections, but nothing too challenging.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: There's a shelter halfway where you can sit and take a break and enjoy the afternoon sunshine.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: And finally Loser's Trail.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: This starts off following a gentle river valley, but the last part is quite exposed, so the snow conditions can be challenging.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: If it's snowing or windy, check with us before you set out to make sure the trail's open that day.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Right, so now if you'd like to follow me, we'll get started.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a woman called Annie, who works at the Snow Center in New Zealand, welcoming a group of visitors to the center.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Hi, everyone.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Welcome to the snow center.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: My name's Annie.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: I hope you enjoyed the bus trip from the airport.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: We've certainly got plenty of snow today.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Well, you've come to New Zealand's premier snow and ski center, and we've a whole load of activities for you during your week here.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Most visitors come here for the cross country skiing.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Where you're on fairly flat ground for most of the time, rather than going down steep mountainsides.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: There are marked trails, but you can also leave these and go off on your own, and that's an experience not to be missed.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: You can go at your own speed.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: It's great aerobic exercise if you really push yourself, or if you prefer, you can just glide gently along and enjoy the beautiful scenery.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: This afternoon you'll be going on a dog sled trip.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: You may have seen our dogs on TV recently, racing in the Winter Sled festival.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: If you want, you can have your own team for the afternoon, and learn how to drive them.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Following behind our leader on the trail, or if you prefer, you can just sit back in the sled and enjoy the ride as a passenger.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: At the weekend, we have the team relay event, and you're all welcome to join in.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: We have a local school coming along, and a lot of the teachers are taking part too.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Participation rather than winning is the main focus, and there's a medal for everyone who takes part.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Participants are in teams of two to four, and each team must complete four laps of the course.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: For your final expedition, you'll head off to Mount Frenner, wearing a pair of special snowshoes which allow you to walk on top of the snow.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: This is an area where miners once searched for gold.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Though there are very few traces of their work left now.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: When the snow melts in summer, the mountain slopes are carpeted in flowers and plants.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: It's a long ascent, though not too steep.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: And walkers generally take a couple of days to get to the summit and return.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: You'll spend the night in our hut halfway up the mountain.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: That's included in your package for the stay.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: It's got cooking facilities, firewood and water for drinking.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: For washing, we recommend you use melted snow though to conserve supplies.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: We can take your luggage up on our snowmobile for you for just $10 a person.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: The hut has cooking facilities, so you can make a hot meal in the evening and morning, but you need to take your own food.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: The weather on Mount Frenner can be very stormy.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: In that case, stay in the hut.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Generally the storms don't last long.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Don't stress about getting back here to the center in time to catch the airport bus.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: They'll probably not be running anyway.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: We do have an emergency locator beacon in the hut, but only use that if it's a real emergency, like if someone's ill or injured.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, let me tell you something about the different ski trails you can follow during your stay here.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Highland Trail's directly accessible from where we are now.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: This trail's been designed to give first-timers an experience they'll enjoy regardless of their age or skill.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: But it's also ideal for experts to practice their technique.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Then there's Pine Trail.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: If you're nervous about skiing, leave this one to the experts.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: You follow a steep valley looking right down on the river below.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Scary.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: But if you fully master the techniques needed for hills, it's great fun.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Stony Trail's a good choice once you've got a general idea of the basics.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: There are one or two tricky sections, but nothing too challenging.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: There's a shelter halfway where you can sit and take a break and enjoy the afternoon sunshine.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: And finally Loser's Trail.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: This starts off following a gentle river valley, but the last part is quite exposed, so the snow conditions can be challenging.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: If it's snowing or windy, check with us before you set out to make sure the trail's open that day.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: Right, so now if you'd like to follow me, we'll get started.
utterances.speaker: ANNIE

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Annie recommends that when cross-country skiing, the visitors should
options: get away from the regular trails.
stop to enjoy views of the scenery.
go at a slow speed at the beginning.

items.title: What does Annie tell the group about this afternoon's dog-sled trip?
options: Those who want to can take part in a race.
Anyone has the chance to drive a team of dogs.
One group member will be chosen to lead the trail.

items.title: What does Annie say about the team relay event?
options: All participants receive a medal.
The course is 4 km long.
Each team is led by a teacher.

items.title: On the snow-shoe trip, the visitors will
options: visit an old gold mine.
learn about unusual flowers.
climb to the top of a mountain.

items.title: The cost of accommodation in the mountain hut includes
options: a supply of drinking water.
transport of visitors' luggage.
cooked meals.

items.title: If there is a storm while the visitors are in the hut, they should
options: contact the bus driver.
wait until the weather improves.
use the emergency locator beacon.

text: What information does Annie give about skiing on each of the following mountain trails?
textReadable: What information does Annie give about skiing on each of the following mountain trails?

list: It has a good place to stop and rest.
It is suitable for all abilities.
It demands a lot of skill.
It may be closed in bad weather.
It has some very narrow sections.
items: Highland Trail <input>
Pine Trail     <input>
Stony Trail    <input>
Loser's Trail  <input>
body.title: Mountain trails

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students called Jack and Alice discussing food labels that give information on the nutritional value of foods.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've still got loads to do for our report on nutritional food labels.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: What did you learn from doing the project about your own shopping habits?
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Well, I've always had to check labels for traces of peanuts in everything I eat because of my allergy.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: But beyond that, I've never really been concerned enough to check how healthy a product is.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: This project has actually taught me to read the labels much more carefully.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I tended to believe claims on packaging like 'low in fat'.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But I now realize that the 'healthy' yogurt I've bought for years is full of sugar, and that it's actually quite high in calories.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Ready meals are the worst.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Comparing the labels on supermarket pizzas was a real eye opener.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Did you have any idea how many calories they contain?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I was amazed.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Yes, because unless you read the label really carefully, you wouldn't know that the nutritional values given are for half a pizza.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: When most people eat the whole pizza.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Not exactly transparent, is it?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Not at all, but I expect it won't stop you from buying pizza.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Probably not, no.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I thought comparing the different labeling systems used by food manufacturers was interesting.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think the kind of labeling system used makes a big difference.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Which one did you prefer?
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I like the traditional daily value system best.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: The one which tells you what proportion of your required daily intake of each ingredient the product contains.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I'm not sure it's the easiest for people to use, but at least you get the full story.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I like to know all the ingredients in a product, not just how much fat, salt and sugar they contain.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: But it's good, supermarkets have been making an effort to provide reliable information for customers.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: There just needs to be more consistency between labeling systems used by different supermarkets, in terms of portion sizes, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Hmm. The labels on the different brands of chicken flavor crisps were quite revealing too, weren't they?
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I don't understand how they can get away with calling them chicken flavor.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: When they only contain artificial additives.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I'd at least have expected them to contain a small percentage of real chicken.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think having nutritional food labelling has been a good idea, don't you?
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I think it will change people's behavior and stop mothers, in particular, buying the wrong things.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But didn't that study kind of prove the opposite?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: People didn't necessarily stop buying unhealthy products.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: They only said that might be the case.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Those findings weren't that conclusive, and it was quite a small-scale study.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I think more research has to be done.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Yes, I think you're probably right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What do you think of the traffic light system?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think supermarkets like the idea of having a color coded system, red, orange or green for levels of fat, sugar and salt in a product.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: But it's not been adopted universally, and not on all products.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Why do you suppose that is?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Pressure from the food manufacturers, hardly surprising that some of them are opposed to flagging up how unhealthy their products are.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I'd have thought it would have been compulsory.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: It seems ridiculous it isn't.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I know, and what I couldn't get over is the fact that it was brought in without enough consultation.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: A lot of experts had deep reservations about it.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: That is a bit weird.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I suppose there's an argument for doing the research now when consumers are familiar with this system.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Yeah, maybe.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: The participants in the survey were quite positive about the traffic light system.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Hmm, but I don't think they targeted the right people.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: They should have focused on people with low literacy levels, because these labels are designed to be accessible to them.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Yeah, but it's good to get feedback from all socio-economic groups.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And there wasn't much variation in their responses.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: No, but if they hadn't interviewed participants face to face, they could have used a much bigger sample size.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: I wonder why they chose that method.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: Dunno. How were they selected?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Did they volunteer, or were they approached?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think they volunteered.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: The thing that wasn't stated was how often they bought packaged food.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: All we know is how frequently they use the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: ALICE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What was Jack's attitude to nutritional food labels before this project?
options: He didn't read everything on them.
He didn't think they were important.
He thought they were too complicated.

items.title: Alice says that before doing this project,
options: she was unaware of what certain foods contained.
she was too lazy to read food labels.
she was only interested in the number of calories.

items.title: When discussing supermarket brands of pizza, Jack agrees with Alice that
options: the list of ingredients is shocking.
he will hesitate before buying pizza again.
the nutritional label is misleading.

items.title: Jack prefers the daily value system to other labelling systems because it is
options: more accessible.
more logical.
more comprehensive.

items.title: What surprised both students about one flavour of crisps?
options: The percentage of artificial additives given was incorrect.
The products did not contain any meat.
The labels did not list all the ingredients.

items.title: What do the students think about research into the impact of nutritional food labelling?
options: It did not produce clear results.
It focused on the wrong people.
It made unrealistic recommendations.

body.title: Labels giving nutritional information on food packaging
Which TWO things surprised the students about the traffic-light system for nutritional labels?
options: its widespread use
the fact that it is voluntary for supermarkets
how little research was done before its introduction
its unpopularity with food manufacturers
the way that certain colours are used

body.title: Which TWO things are true about the participants in the study on the traffic-light system?
options: They had low literacy levels.
They were regular consumers of packaged food.
They were selected randomly.
They were from all socio-economic groups.
They were interviewed face-to-face.

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a presentation by a history student about the history of coffee.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In my presentation, I'm going to talk about coffee, and its importance both in economic and social terms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We think it was first drunk in the Arab world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there's hardly any documentary evidence of it before the 1500s, although of course that doesn't mean that people didn't know about it before then.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, there is evidence that coffee was originally gathered from bushes growing wild in Ethiopia, in the northeast of Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the early 16th century, it was being bought by traders, and gradually its use as a drink spread throughout the Middle East.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's also known that in 1522, in the Turkish city of Constantinople, which was the center of the Ottoman Empire, the court physician approved its use as a medicine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By the mid 1500s, coffee bushes were being cultivated in the Yemen, and for the next hundred years, this region produced most of the coffee drunk in Africa and the Arab world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's particularly interesting about coffee is its effect on social life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was rarely drunk at home, but instead, people went to coffee houses to drink it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These people, usually men, would meet to drink coffee and chat about issues of the day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But at the time, this chance to share ideas and opinions was seen as something that was potentially dangerous, and in 1623, the ruler of Constantinople demanded the destruction of all the coffee houses in the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although after his death, many new ones opened, and coffee consumption continued.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the 17th century, coffee drinking spread to Europe, and here too, coffee shops became places where ordinary people, nearly always men, could meet to exchange ideas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because of this, some people said that these places performed a similar function to universities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The opportunity they provided for people to meet together outside their own homes, and to discuss the topics of the day had an enormous impact on social life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And many social movements and political developments had their origins in coffee house discussions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the late 1600s, the Yemeni monopoly on coffee production broke down, and coffee production started to spread around the world, helped by European colonization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Europeans set up coffee plantations in Indonesia and the Caribbean, and production of coffee in the colonies skyrocketed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Different types of coffee were produced in different areas, and it's interesting that the names given to these different types, like Mocha or Java coffee,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: were often taken from the port they were shipped to Europe from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But if you look at the labor system in the different colonies, there were some significant differences.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Brazil and the various Caribbean colonies, coffee was grown in huge plantations, and the workers there were almost all slaves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But this wasn't the same in all colonies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, in Java, which had been colonized by the Dutch, the peasants grew coffee, and passed a proportion of this onto the Dutch.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it was used as a means of taxation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But whatever system was used, under the European powers of the 18th century, coffee production was very closely linked to colonization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Coffee was grown in ever increasing quantities to satisfy the growing demand from Europe, and it became nearly as important as sugar production, which was grown under very similar conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, coffee prices were not yet low enough for people to drink it regularly at home,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: so most coffee consumption still took place in public coffee houses,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and it still remained something of a luxury item. In Britain, however, a new drink was introduced from China, and started to become popular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Gradually taking over from coffee, although at first it was so expensive that only the upper classes could afford it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was tea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And by the late 1700s it was being widely drunk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, when the USA gained independence from Britain in 1776, they identified this drink with Britain, and coffee remained the preferred drink in the USA, as it still is today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, by the early 19th century, coffee was already being widely produced and consumed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But during this century, production boomed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And coffee prices started to fall.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was partly because new types of transportation had been developed, which were cheaper and more efficient.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So now, working people could afford to buy coffee.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't just a drink for the middle classes, and this was at a time when large parts of Europe were starting to work in industries, and sometimes this meant their work didn't stop when it got dark.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They might have to continue throughout the night.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So the use of coffee as a stimulant became important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't just a drink people drank in the morning for breakfast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There were also changes in cultivation...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a presentation by a history student about the history of coffee.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In my presentation, I'm going to talk about coffee, and its importance both in economic and social terms.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We think it was first drunk in the Arab world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there's hardly any documentary evidence of it before the 1500s, although of course that doesn't mean that people didn't know about it before then.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, there is evidence that coffee was originally gathered from bushes growing wild in Ethiopia, in the northeast of Africa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the early 16th century, it was being bought by traders, and gradually its use as a drink spread throughout the Middle East.
